essays.title: Feed My Addiction
essays.subtitle: The Way of Modern Life.
essays.author: Jevan Goldsmith
essays.content:
In the age of endless scrolling and infinite content, we've become addicted to the very tools designed to set us free. Our smartphones, once heralded as gateways to unlimited knowledge, have become the chains that bind us to a cycle of dopamine-driven consumption.

Every notification is a siren call. Every like, a tiny hit of validation. We've traded deep thinking for quick hits, meaningful conversation for emojis and GIFs. The irony is palpable: in our quest to stay connected, we've never been more alone.

But awareness is the first step. Recognizing our digital addictions doesn't mean abandoning technology altogether. Instead, it means being intentional about our usage. It means choosing depth over breadth, quality over quantity.

The path forward isn't about disconnection—it's about conscious connection. It's about feeding our minds with substance, not just sugar. It's about building relationships that exist beyond the screen.

So ask yourself: What are you feeding? Your addiction, or your growth?

essays.title: The Beginning is the End and the End is the Beginning
essays.subtitle: A case that what matters are only 'when' matters
essays.author: Jevan Goldsmith
essays.content:
Time is circular. Or at least, that's how it often feels when you're building something meaningful. Every ending contains within it the seeds of a new beginning. Every beginning is built on the ashes of what came before.

In business, in life, in relationships—we're constantly cycling through these phases. The trick isn't to avoid the endings or rush to the beginnings. The trick is to recognize that they're the same thing, viewed from different angles.

When a project fails, it's not truly an end. It's the beginning of wisdom. When you start something new, you're not starting from zero—you're starting from everything you've learned.

The question "when" becomes critical here. When do you recognize an ending? When do you embrace a new beginning? These moments of transition are where life happens, where growth occurs.

Most people fear endings because they see them as failures. But what if every ending is just a beginning you haven't recognized yet? What if the timing of your awareness is the only thing that separates success from failure?

The beginning is the end. The end is the beginning. It's all about when you choose to see it.

essays.title: Hot Spas Never go out of Style - A lesson on Culture
essays.author: Jevan Goldsmith
essays.content:
Every great company that has lasted was built on a solid foundation set only by a great culture. There is no single greater force that can put a great business out of business faster than ineffective warm culture.

Think about it: Hot tubs and spas have been around for centuries. From ancient Roman baths to modern luxury resorts, the concept endures. Why? Because the core value proposition never changes—relaxation, rejuvenation, community.

The same principle applies to organizational culture. A truly hot culture—one that's energizing, welcoming, and purposeful—never goes out of style. It attracts talent. It retains people. It drives performance.

But here's the catch: most companies don't have a hot culture. They have a warm one. Comfortable enough that people don't leave immediately, but not exciting enough to inspire greatness. It's the corporate equivalent of a lukewarm bath—nobody wants to stay in it for long.

Warm cultures are dangerous because they're insidious. They feel okay. They don't trigger alarm bells. But over time, they erode motivation, stifle innovation, and create mediocrity.

Building a hot culture requires intention. It means:

  • Being clear about your values and actually living them
  • Creating an environment where people feel psychologically safe to take risks
  • Celebrating wins and learning from failures openly
  • Investing in people's growth, not just their output
  • Having the courage to say no to people who don't fit, even if they're talented

The temperature of your culture is set at the top. Leaders who are lukewarm create lukewarm cultures. Leaders who bring heat—passion, clarity, intensity—create cultures that last.

So ask yourself: Is your organization a hot spa or a warm bath? Because in the long run, only one of those will keep people coming back.

books.title: Atomic Habits
books.author: James Clear
books.shortDescription: A transformative guide to building good habits and breaking bad ones.
books.review:
James Clear's "Atomic Habits" is one of the most practical books on behavior change I've ever read.

The framework is brilliantly simple: tiny changes compound over time. Clear doesn't promise overnight transformation—instead, he shows how 1% improvements, when stacked consistently, lead to remarkable results.

What I loved most:
- The habit loop framework (cue, craving, response, reward) makes abstract concepts concrete
- Real-world examples from athletes, artists, and entrepreneurs
- The focus on systems over goals—a paradigm shift in how I approach personal development

Key takeaway: You don't rise to the level of your goals, you fall to the level of your systems. This idea alone changed how I structure my daily routines.

If you struggle with consistency or want to understand why you do what you do, read this book.

books.title: The Lean Startup
books.author: Eric Ries
books.shortDescription: Essential reading for entrepreneurs on building successful startups.
books.review:
Eric Ries codified what many successful founders learned the hard way: build, measure, learn, repeat.

The core insight is simple but profound: most startups fail not because they can't build a product, but because they build something nobody wants. The solution? Validated learning through rapid experimentation.

The MVP (Minimum Viable Product) concept has been overused and misunderstood since this book, but Ries's original formulation is spot-on: build the smallest thing that lets you start learning.

My favorite chapters focus on pivot vs. persevere decisions. Knowing when to change course is an art, and Ries provides frameworks to make that call more scientific.

If I have one criticism, it's that the book can feel repetitive. The key concepts could have been compressed. But for anyone building a product, this is required reading.

books.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
books.author: Daniel Kahneman
books.shortDescription: A masterpiece on human cognition and decision-making.
books.review:
Kahneman's magnum opus is dense, challenging, and absolutely essential for understanding how humans think.

The dual-system framework—System 1 (fast, intuitive, emotional) and System 2 (slow, deliberate, logical)—explains so much about human behavior. We like to think we're rational, but System 1 is running the show most of the time.

What makes this book brilliant is the decades of rigorous research behind every claim. Kahneman doesn't just theorize; he shows you the experiments, the data, the replication studies.

Key insights that stuck with me:
- Anchoring effects are everywhere and impossible to avoid
- Loss aversion drives more decisions than we realize
- The experiencing self vs. the remembering self explains why we make choices that don't maximize happiness

Warning: This book will make you question every decision you've ever made. You'll start seeing cognitive biases in yourself and others constantly. It's both enlightening and slightly disturbing.

Required reading for anyone interested in psychology, economics, or making better decisions.

adventures.title: Sample Adventure
adventures.subtitle: A placeholder to show how adventures work
adventures.shortDescription: This is a sample adventure to demonstrate the layout. Replace this with your own adventures!
adventures.content:
This is where the full story of your adventure would go. You can write multiple paragraphs here, include photos inline, and really tell the story of your experience.

Add as much detail as you'd like - the places you visited, the people you met, the food you ate, and the memories you made.

adventures.title: Temples & Cherry Blossoms
adventures.subtitle: Exploring ancient Japan during sakura season
adventures.shortDescription: A journey through Japan's ancient capital during the magical cherry blossom season, visiting temples, shrines, and hidden gardens.
adventures.content:
Japan in spring is a dream come true. The cherry blossoms transform every street, park, and temple into a pink wonderland.

Kyoto's thousands of temples and shrines each offer their own unique perspective on Japanese history and spirituality. From the golden pavilion of Kinkaku-ji to the endless torii gates of Fushimi Inari, every day brought new discoveries.

adventures.title: Motorbikes & Mountains
adventures.subtitle: Discovering Vietnam from north to south
adventures.shortDescription: An unforgettable journey through Vietnam's stunning landscapes, from the limestone karsts of Ha Long Bay to the bustling streets of Ho Chi Minh City.
adventures.content:
Vietnam captivated me from the moment I arrived. The energy of the cities, the serenity of the countryside, and the warmth of the people made this an adventure I'll never forget.

From cruising through the emerald waters of Ha Long Bay to exploring ancient temples in Hoi An, every day brought new wonders. The food alone was worth the trip - fresh pho, banh mi, and countless dishes I'd never heard of before.

adventures.title: Harbour City Dreams
adventures.subtitle: Exploring the iconic coastline and vibrant culture of Sydney
adventures.shortDescription: From the iconic Opera House to hidden beaches, discovering why Sydney is one of the world's most beautiful cities.
adventures.content:
Sydney exceeded every expectation. The harbour is even more stunning in person, with the Opera House and Harbour Bridge creating an unforgettable skyline.

Beyond the famous landmarks, I discovered secret coastal walks, vibrant neighborhoods like Newtown and Surry Hills, and some of the freshest seafood I've ever tasted at the Sydney Fish Market.

adventures.title: Red Centre Journey
adventures.subtitle: Walking in the footsteps of ancient traditions
adventures.shortDescription: A spiritual journey to Australia's Red Centre, witnessing Uluru's magic at sunrise and sunset while learning about Aboriginal culture.
adventures.content:
Nothing prepares you for the sheer presence of Uluru. Rising from the flat desert landscape, this sacred monolith changes color throughout the day in ways photographs simply cannot capture.

Learning about the Anangu people's connection to this land over 30,000 years was humbling. The Field of Light art installation at night created a surreal contrast between ancient landscape and modern art.

adventures.title: Live Music & BBQ Capital
adventures.subtitle: Exploring the heart of Texas
adventures.shortDescription: A week in Austin experiencing world-famous BBQ, incredible live music, and the unique culture that makes Texas's capital so special.
adventures.content:
Austin lived up to its reputation as the Live Music Capital of the World. Every night brought new discoveries on 6th Street and beyond.

The BBQ scene here is legendary - from Franklin's to Terry Black's, I sampled brisket that melted in my mouth. Beyond the food and music, the city's tech scene and outdoor activities along Lady Bird Lake made for an unforgettable trip.

adventures.title: Sugar Cane & Sunsets
adventures.subtitle: Discovering the charm of North Queensland
adventures.shortDescription: Exploring the quiet beauty of North Queensland's sugar country, from the Burdekin River to nearby Great Barrier Reef day trips.
adventures.content:
Ayr offered a different side of Australia - the real, working Australia that most tourists never see. Surrounded by endless sugar cane fields, this small town has its own unique charm.

The Burdekin River provided stunning sunset views, and day trips to the nearby reef and Magnetic Island added adventure to the relaxed country pace. The locals were some of the friendliest I've ever met.

featuredQuote.text: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
featuredQuote.author: Aristotle (via Will Durant)

tickerQuotes: Be so good they can't ignore you
Comfort is the enemy of progress
What got you here won't get you there

fullQuotes.text: The mind is everything. What you think you become.
fullQuotes.author: Buddha

fullQuotes.text: First principles thinking is the act of boiling a process down to the fundamental parts that you know are true and building up from there.
fullQuotes.author: Elon Musk

fullQuotes.text: Question assumptions. The things you think are obviously true might not be.
fullQuotes.author: Personal Mantra

fullQuotes.text: Build for decades, not quarters. The best companies are built by people who think in generations.
fullQuotes.author: Personal Mantra

fullQuotes.text: Your margin is my opportunity.
fullQuotes.author: Jeff Bezos

fullQuotes.text: The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now.
fullQuotes.author: Chinese Proverb

fullQuotes.text: Actions reveal priorities. Watch what people do, not what they say.
fullQuotes.author: Personal Mantra

fullQuotes.text: Stay curious, stay humble. The moment you think you know everything is the moment you stop growing.
fullQuotes.author: Personal Mantra

fullQuotes.text: How you do anything is how you do everything.
fullQuotes.author: Unknown

fullQuotes.text: The obstacle is the way. What stands in the way becomes the way.
fullQuotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

fullQuotes.text: We suffer more in imagination than in reality.
fullQuotes.author: Seneca

fullQuotes.text: You have power over your mind - not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
fullQuotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

fullQuotes.text: Be so good they can't ignore you.
fullQuotes.author: Steve Martin

fullQuotes.text: Comfort is the enemy of progress.
fullQuotes.author: P.T. Barnum

fullQuotes.text: What got you here won't get you there.
fullQuotes.author: Marshall Goldsmith

fullQuotes.text: Learn in public. Share your work before you're ready.
fullQuotes.author: Personal Mantra

fullQuotes.text: Compound interest in all things - money, knowledge, relationships, skills.
fullQuotes.author: Personal Mantra

fullQuotes.text: The goal isn't more money. The goal is living life on your terms.
fullQuotes.author: Chris Brogan

fullQuotes.text: Rich is having money. Wealthy is having time.
fullQuotes.author: Unknown

fullQuotes.text: Simple is harder than complex. You have to work hard to get your thinking clean to make it simple.
fullQuotes.author: Steve Jobs

fullQuotes.text: The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask.
fullQuotes.author: Tony Robbins

fullQuotes.text: Culture eats strategy for breakfast.
fullQuotes.author: Peter Drucker

projects.title: Personal Website
projects.shortDescription: This website you're on. A digital home for my thoughts, projects, and adventures.
projects.description: A static personal website evolving into a structured content, product, and agent-readable platform.
projects.whatItIs: A personal archive for essays, projects, books, tools, adventures, and useful notes.
projects.whyIMadeIt: I wanted a durable home for the things I am learning and collecting instead of scattering them across social platforms.
projects.whatILearned: Clear information architecture matters more than adding another page.
projects.nextStep: Tighten the first-visitor path, keep improving project proof, and replace placeholder visuals with owned artifacts.
projects.documentaryLead: This is the live container for the whole archive. The project is less about having a website and more about learning how to make a personal operating system legible to other people.
projects.currentQuestion: How do I make the first five minutes feel like a guided tour instead of a pile of interesting rooms?

proofArtifacts.label: Shipped
proofArtifacts.value: Navigation, search, Field Notes, books, essays, projects, products, adventures, skills, and agent-readable API surfaces.

proofArtifacts.label: Tension
proofArtifacts.value: The archive has more depth than the homepage currently explains.

buildLog.title: Reframed Projects as a build documentary
buildLog.note: Moved project pages away from static portfolio cards and toward current questions, evidence, and dated progress notes.

buildLog.title: Structured the site as a living archive
buildLog.note: Generated pages, metadata, sitemaps, and API outputs so the site can scale without every new thought becoming a hand-edited page.

projects.title: People of History
projects.shortDescription: A deep-dive platform for historical and contemporary figures, linking their works, ideas, influences, and primary sources.
projects.description: A research platform concept for understanding how important figures thought by connecting works, ideas, influences, and source material.
projects.whatItIs: A research concept for mapping people, ideas, works, influences, and source material.
projects.whyIMadeIt: I wanted a better way to study how important people actually thought, not just read disconnected summaries.
projects.whatILearned: The hard part is not collecting more facts; it is designing relationships between them.
projects.nextStep: Define the smallest useful profile format and test it with one historical figure.
projects.documentaryLead: This is a research-system experiment. The bet is that people become easier to understand when their ideas, work, influences, contradictions, and primary sources are shown together.
projects.currentQuestion: What is the smallest profile format that teaches how someone thought without becoming a giant biography?

proofArtifacts.label: Prototype shape
proofArtifacts.value: Person, works, ideas, influences, contradictions, primary sources, and second-order effects.

proofArtifacts.label: Risk
proofArtifacts.value: It can become a fact collection instead of a thinking tool.

buildLog.title: Narrowed the first version
buildLog.note: The next useful version is one excellent profile, not a database of half-finished people.

projects.title: Openclaw Agents
projects.shortDescription: Building AI agents that automate complex workflows and tasks.
projects.description: Exploring the frontier of autonomous systems and practical AI applications through workflow automation agents.
projects.whatItIs: A set of experiments with AI agents that can help run practical workflows.
projects.whyIMadeIt: I want to understand where agents are genuinely useful and where they just add complexity.
projects.whatILearned: Agents need tight scopes, visible feedback, and clear handoff points to stay useful.
projects.nextStep: Document the best agent workflows and turn repeatable patterns into examples.
projects.documentaryLead: This is where I test which agent workflows survive contact with real work. The interesting part is not that an agent can act. It is whether the loop stays visible, bounded, and useful.
projects.currentQuestion: Where do agents save judgment work, and where do they just create a more confident mess?

proofArtifacts.label: Useful pattern
proofArtifacts.value: Agents work better when the task has a clear done state, visible logs, and a human review point.

proofArtifacts.label: Anti-pattern
proofArtifacts.value: Long autonomous loops with vague goals create impressive motion and weak accountability.

buildLog.title: Documenting the handoff points
buildLog.note: The best workflows are the ones where the agent knows when to stop and show its work.

projects.title: Development AI
projects.shortDescription: AI-powered development tools and workflows for accelerating software development and automating coding tasks.
projects.description: A set of experiments around how artificial intelligence can improve software development workflows.
projects.whatItIs: A working set of AI-assisted development workflows for research, coding, review, and QA.
projects.whyIMadeIt: I want to build faster without outsourcing taste, judgment, or responsibility.
projects.whatILearned: The best AI workflow is still a human workflow with better feedback loops.
projects.nextStep: Write up the strongest patterns and connect them to the technical skills archive.
projects.documentaryLead: This project tracks how I actually use AI while building: where it speeds me up, where it makes me lazy, and where the human taste layer still matters most.
projects.currentQuestion: What parts of building should be delegated, and what parts should stay stubbornly human?

proofArtifacts.label: Working loop
proofArtifacts.value: Research, plan, implement, review, QA, then tighten the product judgment.

proofArtifacts.label: Standard
proofArtifacts.value: Speed only counts if the final thing is clearer, more useful, and easier to maintain.

buildLog.title: Separated speed from taste
buildLog.note: The tool can draft, inspect, and verify. It still needs a person deciding what is worth making.

projects.title: Field Notes
projects.shortDescription: A weekly-ish note sharing useful ideas, books, tools, questions, and experiments from the archive.
projects.description: Field Notes is the lightweight dispatch for useful things before they become polished essays.
projects.whatItIs: A short email for ideas, objects, books, questions, and experiments worth keeping.
projects.whyIMadeIt: Some useful things are too small for essays but too valuable to disappear into private notes.
projects.whatILearned: A newsletter needs a clear promise before it needs volume.
projects.nextStep: Publish a sample issue and turn the archive page into the canonical signup surface.
projects.documentaryLead: This is the lightweight publishing channel for things before they become polished essays. It should feel like a useful note from the archive, not a content machine.
projects.currentQuestion: What is the smallest note format that is consistently worth opening?

proofArtifacts.label: Promise
proofArtifacts.value: One useful idea, object, book, question, or experiment from the archive.

proofArtifacts.label: Constraint
proofArtifacts.value: It has to be short enough to send and useful enough to keep.

buildLog.title: Kept the scope small
buildLog.note: The next version needs a sample issue before it needs a cadence.

products.title: MacBook Air M1 2020 16GB
products.shortDescription: The M1 chip with 16GB RAM handles everything I throw at it. Perfect for coding, writing, and running multiple apps simultaneously.
products.description: A daily-driver laptop recommendation for coding, video editing, and heavy multitasking.
products.verdict: The machine I stop thinking about because it just keeps up.
products.whyItStayed: Fast enough for coding, writing, media, and too many browser tabs without becoming the constraint.
products.howIUseIt: Daily work, writing, builds, research, editing, and the general command center for everything digital.
products.replaced: A slower laptop that made context switching feel expensive.

products.title: Galaxy S23 Ultra
products.shortDescription: A big-screen Android phone with an excellent camera system, built-in S Pen, and enough battery to feel dependable all day.
products.description: A flagship Android phone recommendation for capture, notes, and everyday mobile work.
products.verdict: A phone that behaves more like a pocket workstation.
products.whyItStayed: The big screen, strong cameras, and S Pen make it useful for capturing, annotating, and doing real work away from the desk.
products.howIUseIt: Photos, quick notes, reading, navigation, travel planning, and mobile admin when opening a laptop would be overkill.
products.replaced: A smaller phone that made mobile work feel cramped.

products.title: Osmo Action 4
products.shortDescription: Rugged, waterproof action camera with excellent stabilization. Captures adventures without turning the moment into a production.
products.description: An action camera recommendation for travel, adventures, and POV capture.
products.verdict: The pocket camera that makes the best shots actually happen.
products.whyItStayed: Rugged, waterproof, and stabilized enough that the footage looks intentional instead of accidental.
products.howIUseIt: Adventures, travel, POV clips, and the handheld moments where a phone is not the right tool.
products.replaced: Shaky phone footage and shots I kept meaning to get but never did.
